description: New housing development or estate regeneration. Covers social rent, affordable rent, shared ownership, and mixed-tenure schemes.

cost_per_unit_private.note: Construction cost only, excludes land and developer margin

typical_cost.land_cost: Use DLUHC Land Value Estimates by region and existing use class (agricultural: ~GBP 22k/ha, residential: ~GBP 2-6m/ha depending on region)
typical_cost.section_106_cil: Site-specific. Typical S106 affordable housing requirement: 25-40% of units.

typical_benefits.monetisation: DLUHC housing benefit values or hedonic pricing. Difficult to monetise directly; often presented as non-monetised benefit.

typical_benefits.description: Improved housing quality reduces health service use
typical_benefits.monetisation: BRE Housing Health Cost Calculator (2019): average GBP 600/year/household in avoided NHS costs for Category 1 hazard remediation

typical_benefits.description: Direct and supply chain jobs during build
typical_benefits.monetisation: HBF estimate: 1.5 direct FTE jobs per dwelling constructed. Apply IO multiplier for indirect.

typical_benefits.description: New homes generate ongoing council tax receipts
typical_benefits.monetisation: Average Band D council tax by authority (GOV.UK dataset)

typical_benefits.description: CIL/S106 contributions fund schools, healthcare, green space
typical_benefits.monetisation: Value of infrastructure delivered (often treated as cost offset rather than benefit)

typical_benefits.description: Rezoning agricultural to residential land generates uplift
typical_benefits.monetisation: DLUHC Land Value Estimates. NOTE: do not double-count with housing supply value.

deadweight.note: Some housing would be delivered by the market without intervention. Higher deadweight in strong housing markets.

displacement.note: New housing on one site may displace development from another.

leakage.note: Some occupants may come from outside the target area.

reference_bcr_range.note: Housing BCRs are typically modest because the primary benefit (housing itself) is hard to monetise convincingly. Regeneration schemes with significant brownfield remediation or decongestion can score higher.
reference_cases: Homes England Strategic Plan 2023: average BCR for grant-funded affordable housing 1.2-1.8
Northern Powerhouse housing schemes: BCRs typically 1.0-2.5 (MHCLG analysis)
Estate regeneration programmes: BCRs 0.8-1.5 (before non-monetised benefits)
DLUHC Appraisal Guide worked example (affordable housing): BCR 1.4

typical_risks: Planning delays (local opposition, design requirements, affordable housing negotiations)
Construction cost inflation (housing-specific indices often exceed general construction indices)
Developer viability (S106/affordable housing requirements may make schemes unviable)
Ground conditions on brownfield sites (contamination, subsidence)
Housing market downturn reducing cross-subsidy from private sales
Nutrient neutrality requirements (Natural England) in affected catchments
